title: Small Skin Tag on Dog's Chest: Is It Normal?
meta_desc: Noticed a small skin tag on your dog's chest? Learn about acrochordons, why the urgency is low, and how photos help your vet during triage.
content_html:
What is a Skin Tag?

A skin tag, medically known as an acrochordon, is a common, non-cancerous growth often found on dogs. On the chest, these usually appear as small, soft, flesh-colored bumps that may hang off the skin by a thin stalk. They are essentially an overgrowth of skin cells and collagen, frequently occurring in areas of friction or as a dog ages.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for a small, stable skin tag is Low. Most skin tags are purely cosmetic and do not cause your dog any discomfort. You should monitor the growth regularly, but there is typically no need for an emergency visit unless the tag becomes red, inflamed, bleeds, or changes rapidly in size or shape.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photograph is one of the most helpful things you can do for a veterinary professional during a remote triage. A photo allows a vet to see the base of the growth, its texture, and its exact location. This visual information helps differentiate a harmless skin tag from other concerns, such as a tick, a wart, or a potentially more serious skin tumor.

faqs.question: Can I cut a skin tag off my dog at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to remove a skin tag yourself. This can cause significant pain, heavy bleeding, and a high risk of localized infection.

faqs.question: How can I tell if it is a skin tag or a tick?
faqs.answer: A tick will have visible legs near the skin surface if you look closely, whereas a skin tag is a continuous piece of the dog's own fleshy tissue.

faqs.question: When does a skin tag need to be removed?
faqs.answer: Removal is usually only recommended if the skin tag is frequently getting snagged, if the dog is chewing at it, or if it is located in an area where it causes irritation.
